title: Chapter One: The Unseen
content: The dog-eared corner of *Les Fleurs du Mal* was the only part of Maeve Quinn that existed in that room.

She'd folded herself into the back corner of Hamilton Hall's literary salon like a protest — spine pressed to the oak wainscoting, knees drawn up under a black cardigan three sizes too large, the book held high enough to erase her face. The wool smelled of mothballs and someone else's perfume. Vanilla. She'd bought it from a thrift store on Elm Street precisely because it had belonged to a stranger. Anything was better than smelling like herself.

The salon hadn't started yet. Bodies clustered in the center of the room, performing the particular theatre of academic socializing — the performative laugh, the strategic placement of wine glasses, the ancient ritual of deciding who mattered. Maeve watched from behind Baudelaire's corrupted flowers and felt nothing in particular. This was her natural state: the observer, the footnote, the name nobody italicized.
Her phone buzzed against her thigh.
**Chloe 💛:** Running 10 min late. Save me a seat?
Maeve typed back: **There are no seats. It's standing room.**
**Chloe 💛:** Save me a WALL then
She almost smiled. Almost.
The doors at the front of the hall swung open, and the ambient noise shifted — a change in frequency, the particular static that enters a room when beauty arrives.
Chloe Hart walked in like weather.

Golden hair loose on her shoulders. A dress the color of autumn pears that probably cost more than Maeve's semester textbooks. A smile so white and full of voltage that two people near the door actually stepped backward. She moved through the crowd the way light moves through water: effortlessly, catching everything.
And then she broke surface tension entirely, bee-lining across the room to the dark corner where invisible girls lived.

"Maeve." Chloe wedged herself into the gap between Maeve and the wall, breathless, smelling of jasmine and cold October air. "Oh my God, it's packed. I couldn't find parking. I had to park behind the chapel. Behind it, Maeve."
"You're here now."

"I'm here now." Chloe smoothed her dress and performed the quick scan she always did when she entered a space — not checking for admirers, but making sure Maeve was okay. It was so automatic she probably didn't know she did it. "Who's speaking tonight?"
"New hire. Classical lit. Adler."
"Never heard of him."
"Nobody has. It's his first semester."
Chloe leaned into Maeve's shoulder, warm and sure, and a hush ran through the crowd like a current through wire. The podium at the front of the room was no longer empty.
Julian Adler mounted the single step with the economy of someone who resented having to occupy physical space at all.
Maeve's first thought: *That's not fair.*

He was angular in the way of Renaissance sketches — cheekbones that could architecture, a jaw like a chisel's final stroke, the kind of face that made you understand why the Greeks needed a word for beauty that also meant tragedy. Icy, impossible blue. A mouth pressed into a line so austere it seemed designed for cruelty, or for withholding something.
He wore a charcoal crewneck, no tie, sleeves pushed to the forearm. No wedding ring. No watch. Nothing decorative. He was dressed the way a blade is dressed — stripped of anything that doesn't cut.

"Good evening." His voice dropped into the room like cold honey: slow, golden, and somehow capable of making your teeth ache. "I'll be reading from Rilke's letters to a young poet. Not the famous advice — the other parts. The parts where he's uncertain."
A rustle. Disappointment. They'd wanted certainty.
Maeve adjusted her book, her contempt warm and familiar. *Here we go. Another beautiful man reading Rilke like it's a performance piece, using dead artists as personality grafts.*
Then he began.
*"I beg you, to be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart and to try to love the questions themselves."*

His German was perfect. Not the careful perfection of a scholar who'd memorized, but the lived rhythm of someone who'd pressed the words into his bloodstream. He read without drama, without affect — which was its own kind of drama, because the restraint revealed something enormous beneath it.
Maeve felt the hair on her arms rise and hated herself for it.
*No. He's just pretty. Pretty with good diction. This is nothing.*
Chloe's fingers closed around Maeve's forearm like a vise.
"Oh my God," Chloe whispered.
"I know."
"No, Maeve. *Oh my God.*"
"I know, Chloe."

But Chloe wasn't listening. She was rapt, transfixed, her lips slightly parted, her breathing shallow. Maeve had seen Chloe walk red carpets with studied nonchalance. She'd watched her defuse family meltdowns with a single phone call. She had never seen Chloe look like this — like someone had removed her skeleton and she was remaining upright through sheer will.
Julian read on. The second letter. The third. And then he stopped, lowered the book, and spoke without it.

"Rilke tells the young poet to live the questions. But he doesn't say what to do when the questions have teeth." He paused. The silence was absolute. "I think the letters are not advice. I think they're a man trying to write himself out of his own loneliness, and calling it wisdom because the alternative — admitting that even Rilke was simply, terminally alone — was unbearable."
The room held its breath.
And something in Maeve's chest cracked.

Not much. A fissure, a hairline fracture in the thick glass she'd built between herself and the world's beautiful things. It wasn't his face. She'd steeled against faces long ago — the geometry of beauty was just geometry, and geometry couldn't hurt you. It was the precision of it. The originality. He'd taken the most over-read text in the Western canon and found the fault line nobody else had bothered to look for.
*He's not just beautiful. He's brilliant.*
She hated it. She hated it the way you hate a thunderstorm that ruins your plans but makes the air taste electric.

Julian's gaze drifted across the audience — a slow, disinterested sweep, the way someone might scan a menu in a restaurant where nothing sounds good. He passed over Chloe, who was practically vibrating, who was the kind of luminous that usually arrested any room she entered, and his expression didn't flicker. Then his gaze passed over Maeve — or rather, through the space where Maeve was — and landed on the bookshelf behind her.
He hadn't seen her.
Of course he hadn't.

The sting was familiar, a dull needle in old scar tissue. *You don't exist to people like him.* But beneath the sting, threading through it like a second note in a chord — relief. Invisibility was armor. You couldn't wound what you couldn't see.

He finished. Applause, warm and startled, like people clapping after a car accident they'd somehow survived. Julian gave a short nod — not modest, not arrogant, just finished — and stepped down from the podium, disappearing into a knot of approaching faculty before Maeve could track him.
Chloe turned. Her face was flushed, her pupils wide.
"I need to talk to him."
"No."
"Maeve—"
"No." Maeve closed the Baudelaire with more force than necessary. "You don't talk to people like him, Chloe. You smile at them. And that's enough."
"That's not enough."

"It has to be." She shoved the book into her bag. "People like him don't look at people like us. They look *through* us. Like we're glass. Like we're not even furniture — we're the *air* the furniture sits in."
"I don't care."
"You should."
Chloe grabbed Maeve's sleeve. "You're the smartest person I know. If anyone could—"
"I said no."
The words came out harder than she meant. Chloe flinched, and Maeve immediately felt the guilt settle, heavy and damp as the cardigan on her shoulders.
"Chlo. I'm sorry. I just — he's a professor. He's *that* kind of professor. The kind who eats first-year students for breakfast and doesn't remember their names by lunch."
"I don't want him to remember my name."
"Liar."
Chloe's silence was its own kind of confession.

They left Hamilton Hall into the cold. October at Whitmore was a study in extremity — the maples burning themselves out in red, the stone buildings gray and resolute, the night air sharp enough to strip the warmth from your lungs. Chloe walked close beside Maeve, their shoulders bumping, and didn't speak again until they reached the fork where the path split toward their respective dorms.
"You're wrong about him," Chloe said quietly.
"I hope so."
"You're not wrong about most things. But you're wrong about this."

Maeve watched her walk away — golden hair catching the amber of the lamppost, figure shrinking against the dark — and felt something she couldn't name. Not envy. Not exactly. Something closer to the ache of watching someone reach for a live wire and knowing you couldn't stop them.

Maeve lay in bed, her ceiling a blank screen running the evening on loop. The crack in her contempt. The cold honey of his voice. The way his gaze had passed through her like light through an empty room.
Her phone lit up.
**Chloe 💛:** [Screenshot]

It was a faculty page. Whitmore College Department of Classical Literature. Julian Adler, Assistant Professor. Publications listed below his photo — the same face, somehow even more devastating in the flat light of a studio portrait, looking like he resented the camera for existing.
But Chloe hadn't screenshotted the face. She'd screenshotted an essay title.

*"Epistolary Intimacy: Desire, Distance, and the Literature of the Unseen"*
— J. Adler, *Journal of Comparative Literature*, Vol. 44
**Chloe 💛:** He loves LETTERS, Maeve. Not faces. Letters.
Maeve stared at the screen. The glow painted her dark room in cold blue.
*Letters. Not faces.*

She read the title again. *The Literature of the Unseen.* Something clicked. A small, terrible mechanism — the kind that engages before the brain can intervene, the kind that changes the shape of a door from exit to entrance.
Her thumb hovered over the reply field.
She thought: *Don't.*
She thought: *It wouldn't work anyway.*
She thought: *But what if it could?*
The cursor blinked, patient and merciless, and Maeve Quinn — invisible girl, nightshade_ink, believer in nothing so much as her own unlovability — did not reply.
Not yet.
But she didn't put the phone down, either.
